meta.title: Tricks and Trumps
meta.description: How tricks work, following suit, the trump suit

blocks.content:
## The Trick: Bridge's Fundamental Unit

Every card game has a basic unit of action. In poker, it's the hand. In blackjack, it's the round. In bridge, it's the **trick**.

A trick is beautifully simple: four cards, one from each player, played clockwise. One player wins the trick, and that's it. Thirteen tricks per deal, four cards per trick — that accounts for all 52 cards.

But the rules governing which card you can play, and which card wins, create all the strategy.

## How a Trick Works

Here's the step-by-step:

1. **The lead**: One player plays a card face-up on the table. The suit of this card is the **suit led**.
2. **Following suit**: Each subsequent player, going clockwise, **must play a card of the same suit** if they have one. This is not optional — it's a rule.
3. **Can't follow suit?** If you have no cards in the suit led, you may play any card from any other suit.
4. **Winning the trick**: The highest card of the **suit led** wins the trick — unless a trump card was played (more on this shortly).
5. **Leading next**: The winner of the trick leads to the next trick.

## Card Ranking

Within each suit, cards rank from highest to lowest:

> A K Q J 10 9 8 7 6 5 4 3 2

The Ace is always the highest card. The 2 is always the lowest. There are no wild cards and no special combinations — individual card rank is all that matters.

## Following Suit: The Iron Rule

The requirement to follow suit is the most important rule in bridge. Let's make sure it's crystal clear:

- If hearts are led and you have hearts in your hand, you **must** play a heart. You can choose *which* heart, but it must be a heart.
- If hearts are led and you have **zero** hearts, you're "void" in hearts and can play anything.
- You never have to play a high card just because you can. If spades are led and you have ♠A ♠K ♠3, you can play the ♠3 if you want.

Failing to follow suit when you could have is called a **revoke** (or "renege"). It's one of the few rule violations in bridge and carries a penalty.

## A Trick in Notrump

Let's trace through a trick where there's **no trump suit** (a notrump contract):

> **West leads ♦K** — Diamonds are the suit led  
> **North plays ♦5** — Must follow suit (has diamonds)  
> **East plays ♦A** — Must follow suit (and plays the ace!)  
> **South plays ♦2** — Must follow suit  

**Who wins?** East, with the ♦A. The ace is the highest diamond played.

East now leads to the next trick.

## Another Example: Someone Can't Follow Suit

> **North leads ♥Q** — Hearts are the suit led  
> **East plays ♥7** — Following suit  
> **South plays ♥K** — Following suit  
> **West plays ♣9** — Has no hearts! Plays a club instead  

**Who wins?** South, with the ♥K. West's ♣9 doesn't count because clubs weren't the suit led. In notrump, a card from a different suit can never win the trick.

This is a key insight: **in notrump, only cards of the suit led can win**. Cards from other suits are just discards — they're thrown away without effect.

## Enter the Trump Suit

Now things get interesting. When the contract specifies a trump suit (say, spades), that suit has **special power**: a trump card beats any card from any other suit, no matter how high.

The rules for following suit don't change — you still must follow the suit led if you can. But when you **can't** follow suit, you have a choice:

1. **Trump** (also called **ruff**): Play a card from the trump suit. This beats all non-trump cards.
2. **Discard**: Play a card from a non-trump, non-led suit. This card cannot win the trick.

## A Trick With Trumps

Let's say the contract is **4♠** (spades are trumps):

> **West leads ♥A** — Hearts are the suit led  
> **North plays ♥3** — Following suit  
> **East plays ♥8** — Following suit  
> **South plays ♠2** — Has no hearts! Trumps with the ♠2  

**Who wins?** South! Even though West played the ♥A (highest heart), South's lowly ♠2 wins because **a trump card beats any non-trump card**, regardless of rank.

This is the magic of trumps. A tiny trump card can beat an ace of another suit.

## When Multiple Players Trump

If two players both play trump cards on the same trick, the **higher trump** wins:

> **North leads ♦J** — Diamonds are the suit led  
> **East plays ♠5** — No diamonds, trumps with ♠5  
> **South plays ♦4** — Following suit  
> **West plays ♠K** — No diamonds, overtrumps with ♠K  

**Who wins?** West, with the ♠K. Both East and West trumped, but ♠K beats ♠5.

## The Hierarchy of Power

To summarize which card wins a trick:

| Situation | Winner |
|-----------|--------|
| No trumps played | Highest card of the suit led |
| One trump played | The trump card |
| Multiple trumps played | The highest trump card |

Cards from non-led, non-trump suits **never win**. They're pure discards.

## Notrump vs. Suit Contracts

The contract determines whether there's a trump suit:

- **Suit contract** (e.g., 4♠, 3♥, 5♦, 2♣): The named suit is trump. Cards of that suit have the power to beat any other suit.
- **Notrump contract** (e.g., 1NT, 3NT): There is no trump suit. The highest card of the suit led always wins.

Notrump contracts play very differently from suit contracts. In notrump, the side with long, established suits has a big advantage because there's no way for the opponents to trump in and steal tricks. In suit contracts, even a player with only small cards can win tricks by trumping.

This difference is fundamental and shapes everything from bidding strategy to card play technique.

blocks.title: Trace through this trick. Spades are trumps. North leads the ♥K. East plays ♥3. South plays ♥A. West plays ♠7. Who wins this trick?
blocks.solution: West wins with the ♠7. Even though South played the ♥A (the highest heart), West trumped with ♠7. A trump card beats any non-trump card, regardless of rank. So West's little ♠7 beats South's ♥A.

blocks.content: A trick is four cards, one from each player. You must follow the suit led if you can. In notrump, the highest card of the suit led wins. In a suit contract, a trump card beats any non-trump card — and if multiple trumps are played, the highest trump wins.

reviewQuestions.question: What must you do if you hold cards in the suit that was led?
options: Play your highest card in that suit
Play any card from that suit
reviewQuestions.explanation: You must follow suit (play a card from the suit led), but you can choose which card of that suit to play.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly! You must follow suit, but you pick which card. No obligation to play high.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: You must play a card from the suit led -- that's the iron rule. But you choose which one. You don't have to play your highest.

reviewQuestions.question: In a notrump contract, can a card from a different suit ever win the trick?
options: Yes, if it's the highest card played
reviewQuestions.explanation: In notrump, only cards of the suit led can win. Cards from other suits are discards with no trick-winning power.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right! In notrump, only the suit led matters. Everything else is a throwaway.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: In notrump, only cards of the suit that was led can win the trick. A card from any other suit is just a discard, even if it's an ace.
